title: Exploring Simpson’s paradox within the Penguin dataset
text:
NoteA few consideration about this doc





This Quarto document serves as a practical illustration of the concepts covered in the productive workflow online course. It’s designed primarily for educational purposes, so the focus is on demonstrating Quarto techniques rather than on the rigor of its scientific content.

title: Exploring Simpson’s paradox within the Penguin dataset
text:
1 Introduction
This document offers a straightforward analysis of the well-known penguin dataset. You could read about this new fantastic dataset here.
Let’s load the libraries now!


Show the code
# Load packages
library(tidyverse)
library(patchwork)
library(tinytex)
library(DT)
library(knitr)
library(plotly)
library(hrbrthemes)

title: Exploring Simpson’s paradox within the Penguin dataset
text:
2 Loading data and function
Here we are loading already cleaned data, that we previously processed. The original format was exls. Let’s load that clean version, with a few functions available in functions.R.


Show the code
# Source functions
source(file = "functions.R")
# Read the clean dataset
data <- readRDS(file = "../input/clean_data.rds")


Note that bill_length_mm and bill_depth_mm have the following signification.



Bill measurement explanation


In case you’re wondering how the original dataset looks like, here is a searchable version of it, made using the DT package:


Show the code
datatable(data, filter="top")

title: Exploring Simpson’s paradox within the Penguin dataset
text:
3 Bill length and depth
Now, let’s make some descriptive analysis, including summary statistics and graphs. What’s striking is the slightly negative relationship between bill lengthandbill depth:


Show the code
#Custom theme
AnastasiyaP_theme <- function() {
  theme_ipsum() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(color = "#29266D", hjust=0.5, size = 16, face = "bold"),
      axis.text.x = element_text(size = 8),
      axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8),
      legend.position = "none",
      axis.line.x = element_line(size = 0.5, colour = "grey40", linetype=1),
      axis.line.y = element_line(size = 0.5, colour = "grey40", linetype=1),
      plot.margin = margin(20,20,30,20)
    )
}

p <- data %>%
  ggplot(
    aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm)
  ) +
  geom_point(color = "#69b3a2") +
  labs(
    x = "Bill length",
    y = "Bill depth",
    title = paste("Surprising relationship?")
  ) +
  AnastasiyaP_theme() 

ggplotly(p)

title: Exploring Simpson’s paradox within the Penguin dataset
text:
4 Split your data by group
Maybe it’s Simpson’s paradox1? Let’s look at the plot by species.


Show the code
col_vec <- c(
  "Adelie" = "#FFAC31",
  "Chinstrap" = "#777CD3",
  "Gentoo" = "#343188"
)

pp <- data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_manual(values = col_vec) +
  labs(
    x = "Bill length",
    y = "Bill depth",
    title = "There isn't surprising relationship"
  ) +
  AnastasiyaP_theme() 

ggplotly(pp)






It is also interesting to note that bill length and bill depth are quite different from one specie to another.
The average of a variable can be computed as follow: \[{\displaystyle Avg={\frac {1}{n}}\sum _{i=1}^{n}a_{i}={\frac {a_{1}+a_{2}+\cdots +a_{n}}{n}}}\] The average’s of length and depth by species are summarized in the table below:


Show the code
bill_length_depth_mean <-  data %>%
  group_by(species) %>%
  summarise(mean_length = round(mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
            mean_depth = round(mean(bill_depth_mm, na.rm = TRUE), 1))

# Display in the Quarto report
kable(bill_length_depth_mean)





species
mean_length
mean_depth




Adelie
38.8
18.3


Chinstrap
48.8
18.4


Gentoo
47.5
15.0





Show the code
# Calculating specie's specific mean 
bill_length_adelie <- data %>%
  group_by(species) %>% 
  summarise(average_bill_length = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm= TRUE)) %>% 
  filter(species == "Adelie") %>% 
  pull(average_bill_length) %>% 
  round(1)


For instance, the average bill length for the specie Adelie is 38.8.
Now, let’s check the relationship between bill depth and bill length for each species separately.


Show the code
# Use the function in functions.R
p1 <- create_scatterplot(data, "Adelie", col_vec)+
  AnastasiyaP_theme()+theme(axis.title.x = element_blank())
p2 <- create_scatterplot(data, "Chinstrap", col_vec)+ 
  AnastasiyaP_theme()+theme(axis.title.x = element_blank())
p3 <- create_scatterplot(data, "Gentoo", col_vec)+ 
  AnastasiyaP_theme()+theme(axis.title.x = element_blank())

(p1 + p2 + p3)+
  plot_annotation(
    caption = "Bill length (mm)"
  ) &
  theme(plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0.5))





There is relationship between bill length and bill depth, when you split data by species.

title: Exploring Simpson’s paradox within the Penguin dataset
text:
5 Conclusion
Thus we’ve realize three distinct group of penguin. Within each of them we see positive correlation between bill length and bill depth unlike without splitting by groups as shown in section 3

title: Exploring Simpson’s paradox within the Penguin dataset
text:
Footnotes


Simpson’s paradox, or the Yule–Simpson effect, is a phenomenon in probability and statistics, in which a trend appears in several different groups of data but disappears or reverses when these groups are combined.read more here↩︎
